summary: How HubSpot solved the two-date problem (1 minute read) Showing both publish and updated dates can hurt CTR if Google surfaces the older one in search results. This keeps search listings looking fresh while still giving readers context, helping maintain SEO performance.

summary: Claude Code and Cowork can now use your computer (1 minute read) Claude Code and Claude Cowork can now open files, use browsers, and run dev tools on users' computers. Claude computer use is initially available to Claude Pro and Claude Max subscribers on macOS.

summary: OpenAI rolls out ChatGPT Library to store your personal files (2 minute read) ChatGPT's new Library feature allows users to store personal files or images on OpenAI's cloud storage. summary: Designing AI Chip Hardware and Software (Book) The future of AI accelerators will be AI CPUs - traditional CPUs with big caches and systolic arrays, where the caches and systolic arrays take up most of the chip area and power. This book goes over what is required to design a competitive AI chip. The recommendations made in the book will require an excellent software team to realize, but the topics covered should be easily understandable at a hobbyist level.

title: Agency
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-25, Reading time: 11 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2FjDMTk2/1/0100019d2485775b-614790a6-4cd3-44a0-92c4-c28dc59e78aa-000000/4BFRQErcnClbHzg9SGruZO3lq_pLHuDJgNcQvkrJcrc=450
summary: Agency (11 minute read) Agency is the ability to hold the entire development loop in your head and act on it. A high-agency person can move through the whole sequence without waiting for someone else to hand them the next step. Agency is what you call it when one person can cover the full distance. What matters is that the whole thing exists in a user's hands, and that it was driven there quickly by one person's judgment.

title: How Agentic RAG Works
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-25, Reading time: 12 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fblog.bytebytego.com%2Fp%2Fhow-agentic-rag-works%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019d24b03cf4-6c41655b-5ce2-41b1-b45a-f40151fc59af-000000/xJS6aC61ir4otmkeff1TtF_XkB16ybIDZeo2cys9W74=449
summary: How Agentic RAG Works (12 minute read) Standard RAG is a one-shot pipeline: query in, retrieve, generate. However, it breaks down the moment a question is ambiguous, the answer is spread across multiple sources, or the retrieved chunk looks relevant but isn't. Agentic RAG fixes this by adding an LLM in the middle that can rewrite the query, route to the right source, evaluate whether what came back is actually good enough, and loop again if not.

title: Node.js worker threads are problematic, but they work great for us
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-25, Reading time: 13 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.inngest.com%2Fblog%2Fnode-worker-threads%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019d24b03cf4-6c41655b-5ce2-41b1-b45a-f40151fc59af-000000/H8sA5AZ9coHJSHxR-fOieVNS6vXa64hieK0ttw4pD-w=449
summary: Node.js worker threads are problematic, but they work great for us (13 minute read) Inngest encountered event loop starvation in its Node.js-based Connect service, where CPU-heavy user code blocked critical heartbeats, causing "no available worker" errors. Their solution was to move the Connect service's internals, like WebSocket connections and heartbeats, into a separate Node.js worker thread to isolate them from user code execution.

summary: Code as a Tool of Process (3 minute read) Programming is an iterative process that sharpens one's understanding by confronting intricate details and questions as features are built. However, generating code with AI skips this sharpening process, preventing developers from deeply understanding the complexities and trade-offs involved.

title: The Death of model.fit: What Data Scientists Actually Do in the Age of AI Agents
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-25, Reading time: 12 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fengineering.monday.com%2Fthe-death-of-model-fit-what-data-scientists-actually-do-in-the-age-of-ai-agents%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019d24b03cf4-6c41655b-5ce2-41b1-b45a-f40151fc59af-000000/UPlH7f-o9WQytfTfkClfkl_nQLRkZSDHqqBEtGA8PxY=449
summary: The Death of model.fit(): What Data Scientists Actually Do in the Age of AI Agents (12 minute read) In agentic AI systems, data scientists don't train models. Rather, they own the evaluation infrastructure that keeps agent behavior measurable and improvable. That means building error taxonomies from production traces, maintaining golden datasets of real user inputs, calibrating LLM-as-judge against human raters, and gating deploys with deterministic schema checks.

summary: Design Patterns for Building Trust (6 minute read) Contrary to common assumptions, the safest path to user trust in AI isn't maximum transparency, but calibrated trust. Design patterns such as scoped prompting, manual overrides, and AI disclosure help build trust across five psychological foundations.

summary: After hackers hit an Iowa company, cars around the country failed to start (3 minute read) A cyberattack hit Intoxalock on March 14, taking down the backend systems that DUI-convicted drivers depend on to calibrate their court-mandated ignition interlock devices. comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-26, Reading time: 12 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.greybeam.ai%2Fblog%2Fwhy-your-joins-are-slow%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019d299ee2b7-1c910ad2-a037-4d9c-aec8-7615d2384dc7-000000/_oIeNixHQwhmYzw7L7vvLxqv5HlDTmP3-NTqcBuwZjY=450
summary: Why Your Snowflake Joins Are Slow: Fix OR Joins Fast (12 minute read) Disjunctive joins (using OR in join conditions) break Snowflake's hash join optimization, forcing expensive Cartesian products and massive performance slowdowns. The fix is to rewrite them as separate equi-joins, restoring efficient execution and often delivering 100–200x speedups.

title: When upserts don't update but still write: Debugging Postgres performance at scale
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-26, Reading time: 11 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.datadoghq.com%2Fblog%2Fengineering%2Fdebugging-postgres-performance%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019d299ee2b7-1c910ad2-a037-4d9c-aec8-7615d2384dc7-000000/ngx90QKq8yDT95OmeU_LoeCefP61WCAObe3dN1zj-qc=450
summary: When upserts don't update but still write: Debugging Postgres performance at scale (11 minute read) Datadog hit a surprising Postgres performance issue while cleaning up millions of ephemeral hosts: a simple upsert to update the "last seen" timestamp doubled disk writes and quadrupled WAL syncs. The cause was ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE always acquires a row lock and writes to the WAL, even when no data actually changes. The fix is to avoid locking on no-op upserts.

title: How Anthropic's Claude Thinks
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-26, Reading time: 15 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fblog.bytebytego.com%2Fp%2Fhow-anthropics-claude-thinks%3Futm_source=tldrnewsletter/1/0100019d29aec9d6-abb4feed-7768-4ae8-be15-1e8bb30546e8-000000/VNiihzRaMBvN88_tQPk8BNoMHf3w8h7KNeHmNlAozbk=450
summary: How Anthropic's Claude Thinks (15 minute read) Claude was trained on data, and it developed its own strategies, so the people who built it didn't really know how it worked. Anthropic built a set of tools to trace the actual computational steps the model takes to produce an answer. Its team traced Claude's internal computations across tasks over the course of multiple research papers published in 2025. This article reviews these papers to see what the Claude researchers found.

title: Google bumps up Q Day deadline to 2029, far sooner than previously thought
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-26, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Farstechnica.com%2Fsecurity%2F2026%2F03%2Fgoogle-bumps-up-q-day-estimate-to-2029-far-sooner-than-previously-thought%2F%3Futm_source=tldrnewsletter/1/0100019d29aec9d6-abb4feed-7768-4ae8-be15-1e8bb30546e8-000000/k39HpbKITtR1rWYYW7SCpjr6aXRNQOt06OJIrMB88d0=450
summary: Google bumps up Q Day deadline to 2029, far sooner than previously thought (5 minute read) Q Day is the point at which existing quantum computers can break public-key cryptography algorithms. Everyone should prepare by adopting post-quantum cryptography (PQC) algorithms. Google is now giving itself until 2029 to prepare for the event. The beta version of Android 17 will add support for developers to have PQC keys for signing their apps and verifying other software signatures.

title: Notes from the SaaS Funeral
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-03-26, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2FzxvXLe/1/0100019d29aec9d6-abb4feed-7768-4ae8-be15-1e8bb30546e8-000000/MSY6lUlNT2bj9oe-4GHjVCsNhDTHX5m_uuS27m5P2QU=450
summary: Notes from the SaaS Funeral (5 minute read) The moat around SaaS has weakened. However, a reduction of margins is not the same as being dead. Companies are unlikely to be able to prompt their way to a full functional HR system, accounts payable platform, or enterprise CRM anytime soon. Software businesses are living systems that require maintenance, verification, security, compliance, and ongoing refinement.

title: Why I Love the Constraints of Email

summary: Why I Love the Constraints of Email (5 minute read) Email's power comes from its constraints. A narrow 600px layout, outdated HTML, and unpredictable rendering force focus on clarity, strong copy, and intentional design instead of flashy features. These limits reduce decision fatigue and spark creativity, pushing marketers to prioritize the most important message. With fewer distractions and a defined structure, emails deliver higher ROI and conversions than more technically capable channels.
